title: Kth largest stream
description: Find kth largest in infinite stream using heap

examples.explanation: Initialize list = []. Add 5: list = [5]. Add 3: list = [5, 3]. Add 8: list = [5, 3, 8]. Add 2: list = [5, 3, 8, 2]. Query k=2: sort list descending -> [8, 5, 3, 2], return element at index 1 -> 5. Query k=3: list still [5, 3, 8, 2], sort descending -> [8, 5, 3, 2], return index 2 -> 3.

examples.input: Stream: [10, 20, 30], Queries: k=1, k=2 after each addition
examples.explanation: Start list = []. Add 10: list = [10]. Query k=1: sort [10] -> [10], return index 0 -> 10. Query k=2: invalid since length=1, return error or None. Add 20: list = [10, 20]. Query k=1: sort [10,20] descending -> [20,10], return index 0 -> 20. Query k=2: same sorted list, return index 1 -> 10. Add 30: list = [10,20,30]. Query k=1: sort [10,20,30] descending -> [30,20,10], return index 0 -> 30. Query k=2: same sorted list, return index 1 -> 20.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n) per query
approaches.summary: Maintain a list of all stream elements seen so far. For each query to find the kth largest, sort the list in descending order and return the element at index k-1. This is straightforward but inefficient for frequent queries.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Kth Largest in Infinite Stream via Brute Force
ladders.desc: Implement a class that maintains all stream elements and returns kth largest via sorting for each query.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the kth largest element in an infinite stream involves maintaining a complete history of all elements received so far. For each query asking for the kth largest, we sort the entire list of accumulated elements in descending order and return the element at position k-1 (since indexing typically starts at 0). This method is intuitive because sorting naturally orders elements by value, making it trivial to find any rank-based statistic. However, it's inefficient for frequent queries because each query requires O(n log n) time where n is the number of elements seen so far. The intuition is simple: by keeping all data, we can always recompute the kth largest from scratch when asked. The algorithm works as follows: initialize an empty list to store stream elements. For each new element from the stream, append it to the list. When a query arrives with parameter k, sort the list in descending order, check that k is valid (1 ≤ k ≤ list length), then return list[k-1]. Time complexity is O(n log n) per query due to sorting, and space complexity is O(n) to store all elements. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, working solution. Common mistakes include not handling invalid k values, forgetting to sort in descending order, or inefficiently sorting the entire list repeatedly. The real code pattern involves a class with add() and getKthLargest() methods.

examples.trace: Initialize list = []. Add 5: list = [5]. Add 3: list = [5, 3]. Add 8: list = [5, 3, 8]. Add 2: list = [5, 3, 8, 2]. Query k=2: sort list descending -> [8, 5, 3, 2], return element at index 1 -> 5. Query k=3: list still [5, 3, 8, 2], sort descending -> [8, 5, 3, 2], return index 2 -> 3.

examples.input: Stream: [10, 20, 30], Queries: k=1, k=2 after each addition
examples.trace: Start list = []. Add 10: list = [10]. Query k=1: sort [10] -> [10], return index 0 -> 10. Query k=2: invalid since length=1, return error or None. Add 20: list = [10, 20]. Query k=1: sort [10,20] descending -> [20,10], return index 0 -> 20. Query k=2: same sorted list, return index 1 -> 10. Add 30: list = [10,20,30]. Query k=1: sort [10,20,30] descending -> [30,20,10], return index 0 -> 30. Query k=2: same sorted list, return index 1 -> 20.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sorting List to Find Kth Largest
ladders.desc: Given a list and k, sort it in descending order and return the kth largest element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: taking an unsorted list of numbers and a rank k, we sort the list in descending order to directly access the kth largest element at index k-1. The intuition is that sorting transforms the problem from searching through unordered data to simple array indexing. By arranging elements from largest to smallest, the first element is the largest, second is second-largest, and so on. This works because sorting is a well-defined operation that orders elements based on their values. The algorithm: accept a list and integer k as input. First, validate that k is between 1 and the length of the list (inclusive). If invalid, return an error or sentinel value. Then, sort the list in descending order using any sorting algorithm (e.g., built-in sort with reverse=True). Finally, return the element at position k-1 in the sorted list. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to sorting, where n is list length, and space complexity is O(n) for the sorted copy or O(1) if sorted in-place. This feeds into the full problem by being the core operation performed on the accumulated stream data for each query. Common mistakes include using ascending sort and accessing from the end (which is error-prone), not handling edge cases like empty lists or k out of bounds, and inefficiently sorting for each call without caching. The real code pattern is a function that takes list and k, sorts, and returns value.

examples.trace: Input list = [4,2,7,1], k=2. Validate: k=2 is valid since 1 ≤ 2 ≤ 4. Sort list descending: [7,4,2,1]. Return element at index k-1 = 1: value 4.
Input list = [10,20,30], k=1. Validate: k=1 is valid (1 ≤ 1 ≤ 3). Sort descending: [30,20,10]. Return index 0: value 30.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining All Stream Elements in a List
ladders.desc: Accumulate every element from an infinite stream into a growing list for later processing.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is to store every element received from the stream in a data structure (a list) as they arrive. This accumulation allows us to have complete historical data available whenever a query for the kth largest is made. The intuition is that by preserving all data, we can perform any computation on the entire dataset at query time, ensuring correctness regardless of stream order or frequency. This works because the stream is infinite and we need to consider all elements seen so far to determine ranks accurately. The algorithm involves initializing an empty list. For each new element from the stream, append it to the list. This operation is O(1) amortized time per addition. The list grows dynamically as more elements arrive. The space complexity is O(n) where n is the number of elements added. This connects to the key sub-routine by providing the raw data that will be sorted when a query occurs. It's a simple yet crucial step: without storing all elements, we couldn't sort them later. Common mistakes include using a fixed-size array (which can overflow), not handling stream termination (though it's infinite in theory), or inefficiently storing duplicates (which is fine here). The real code pattern is to have an add method that appends to a list.

examples.trace: Start with empty list []. Receive 5: append -> [5]. Receive 3: append -> [5,3]. Receive 8: append -> [5,3,8]. Final list is [5,3,8].
Start list []. Add 10: [10]. Add 20: [10,20]. Add 30: [10,20,30]. Add 20: [10,20,30,20]. Note duplicates are kept.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting a List in Descending Order
ladders.desc: Sort a list of numbers from largest to smallest using a sorting algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves taking an unsorted list of numbers and rearranging it so that elements are in descending order (largest first). This is a fundamental operation needed to efficiently find rank-based statistics like the kth largest. The intuition is that by ordering elements, we transform a search problem into an indexing problem. Sorting works by comparing elements and swapping them until the entire list is ordered. For the brute force approach, we can use any comparison-based sorting algorithm like quicksort, mergesort, or built-in sort functions. The algorithm: given a list, apply a sorting algorithm with a comparator that sorts in descending order (e.g., for built-in sort, use reverse=True). The result is a new list or the original list modified in-place. Time complexity is O(n log n) for efficient sorts, and space complexity can be O(n) for algorithms that use extra space or O(1) for in-place sorts. This connects to the core logic by being the operation performed on the accumulated list when a query is made. It's essential because without sorting, finding the kth largest would require more complex logic. Common mistakes include sorting in ascending order by accident, not handling empty or single-element lists, or using inefficient O(n²) sorts for large datasets. The real code pattern is sorted_list = sorted(original_list, reverse=True) or original_list.sort(reverse=True).

examples.trace: Input list [3,1,4,2]. Apply descending sort: compare and rearrange. Steps: identify largest 4, then 3, then 2, then 1. Sorted list is [4,3,2,1].
Input [5,5,3]. Sort descending: elements are already in order when considering duplicates. Sorted list remains [5,5,3] since 5 ≥ 5 ≥ 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Appending to a Dynamic List
ladders.desc: Add a new element to the end of a list, increasing its size.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves adding a new element to a dynamic list (like Python's list or Java's ArrayList) at the end. It's fundamental for accumulating stream data. The intuition is that lists are sequential containers that can grow as needed, making them ideal for storing an unknown number of stream elements. Appending works by placing the new element in the next available position in memory, and if the list's capacity is exceeded, it dynamically resizes (usually doubling in size) to accommodate more elements. This ensures amortized O(1) time complexity per append. The algorithm: given a list and a new element, use the append method (e.g., list.append(element) in Python) to add it to the end. The list's length increases by one. Space complexity increases by O(1) per element, but overall O(n) for n elements. This connects to the building block by being how we populate the list that will later be sorted. It's a simple yet critical operation for data collection. Common mistakes include using fixed-size arrays without resizing (leading to overflow), inserting at the beginning (which is O(n)), or not updating the list reference properly. The real code pattern is my_list.append(new_value).

examples.trace: Start with list [1,2]. Append 3: add 3 to the end. List becomes [1,2,3]. Length increases from 2 to 3.
Start with empty list []. Append 5: list becomes [5]. Length increases from 0 to 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Lists and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what a list is and how to access elements by position (index).
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces lists (also called arrays or sequences) as ordered collections of elements where each element has a position called an index. Understanding this is prerequisite for the brute force approach because we store stream elements in a list and access them by index after sorting. The intuition is that lists allow us to group multiple values together and retrieve them based on their order. In most programming languages, indexing starts at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, and so on. To access an element, use the syntax list[index]. For example, if list = [10,20,30], then list[0] is 10, list[1] is 20. Lists are dynamic in languages like Python, meaning they can grow and shrink. This works because memory is allocated contiguously or via references. Time complexity for indexing is O(1) since it's a direct memory access. Space complexity is O(n) to store n elements. This connects to the basic operation because appending adds elements to the end of a list, and later we'll index into it. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in math but not programming), accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., index 3 in a list of length 3), or confusing lists with other data structures. The real code pattern involves creating a list, adding elements, and accessing via indices.

examples.trace: List has elements: index 0 -> 5, index 1 -> 3, index 2 -> 7. Access index 1: return value 3.
List has one element at index 0: value 10. Access index 0: return 10.

approaches.name: Min-Heap O(log k) per insertion
approaches.summary: Use a min-heap of size k to store the k largest elements from the stream. For each new element, if the heap size is less than k, add it; otherwise, compare with the heap's root (smallest of the k largest) and replace if the new element is larger. The kth largest is always at the root.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log k) per insertion, O(1) per query

ladders.title: Full Problem — Kth Largest in Infinite Stream with Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding kth largest element in an infinite stream using a min-heap of size k.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires maintaining the kth largest element from an infinite stream of numbers in real-time. The key insight is that we only need to track the k largest elements seen so far, and among those, the smallest one (the root of a min-heap) will be the kth largest overall. We initialize an empty min-heap. For each new element from the stream: if the heap size is less than k, we push the element. If the heap is already at size k, we compare the new element with the heap's root (the current kth largest). If the new element is larger, we pop the root and push the new element; otherwise, we ignore it. The kth largest is always available at the root in O(1) time. The intuition is that by keeping exactly k elements and always removing the smallest when a larger one arrives, we ensure the heap contains the k largest elements seen. Each insertion takes O(log k) time for heap operations, and space is O(k) to store the heap. This approach is optimal for this streaming scenario because we process elements one by one without storing the entire stream. Common mistakes include not handling the initial phase when heap size < k, incorrectly using a max-heap, or not updating the heap when a new element is equal to the root (we only replace if larger to maintain exact kth largest). The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the addNumber subroutine repeatedly for each stream element.

examples.trace: Initialize empty min-heap. Stream: 4 -> heap size 0 < 3, push 4 -> heap [4]. 5 -> size 1 < 3, push 5 -> heap [4,5]. 8 -> size 2 < 3, push 8 -> heap [4,5,8], root=4 (3rd largest). 2 -> size 3 = k, compare 2 with root 4, 2 <= 4, ignore -> heap unchanged, root=4. 3 -> compare 3 with root 4, 3 <= 4, ignore -> root=4. 5 -> compare 5 with root 4, 5 > 4, pop root 4, push 5 -> heap [5,5,8], root=5. 10 -> compare 10 with root 5, 10 > 5, pop root 5, push 10 -> heap [5,8,10], root=5. 9 -> compare 9 with root 5, 9 > 5, pop root 5, push 9 -> heap [8,9,10], root=8. 4 -> compare 4 with root 8, 4 <= 8, ignore -> final heap [8,9,10], root=8 is 3rd largest. But wait, after processing 10, heap was [5,8,10] with root 5, then 9 replaced 5 making heap [8,9,10] with root 8, and 4 ignored. So final kth largest is 8? Let's verify: sorted elements: [2,3,4,4,5,5,8,9,10], 3rd largest is 8. Yes, correct.

Initialize empty min-heap. 1 -> heap size 0 < 2, push 1 -> heap [1]. 2 -> size 1 < 2, push 2 -> heap [1,2], root=1 (2nd largest). 3 -> size 2 = k, compare 3 with root 1, 3 > 1, pop root 1, push 3 -> heap [2,3], root=2. 4 -> compare 4 with root 2, 4 > 2, pop root 2, push 4 -> heap [3,4], root=3. 5 -> compare 5 with root 3, 5 > 3, pop root 3, push 5 -> heap [4,5], root=4. Final kth largest is 4.

testCases.input: k=10, stream: [i for i in range(1,21)]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Add Number to Heap and Maintain k Largest
ladders.desc: Function to process a new stream element and update the min-heap to maintain k largest elements.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the streaming algorithm. Given a min-heap (implemented as a list with heap operations) that currently holds up to k elements (the k largest seen so far), and a new number from the stream, we need to update the heap appropriately. The intuition is that to find the kth largest, we only care about the k largest numbers; if a new number is among them, it should be in the heap, and if not, we ignore it. Since the heap is a min-heap of size at most k, its root is the smallest of the k largest, i.e., the current kth largest. Algorithm: if the heap size is less than k, simply push the new number. If the heap size is already k, compare the new number with the root. If new number > root, then the new number belongs in the top k, so we pop the root (the smallest of the current top k) and push the new number. If new number <= root, ignore it. This ensures the heap always contains the k largest elements seen, with the root as the kth largest. Time complexity is O(log k) for heap push/pop operations. Space is O(k) for the heap. Common mistakes include pushing without checking heap size first (leading to heap size > k), or not handling the comparison correctly (e.g., replacing when new number is equal to root, which would change the kth largest unnecessarily). This subroutine feeds into L0 by being called for each stream element. Real code pattern: def addNumber(heap, num, k): if len(heap) < k: heapq.heappush(heap, num); else: if num > heap[0]: heapq.heapreplace(heap, num).

examples.input: heap initially [4,5,8] (min-heap, root=4), k=3, new number=5
examples.trace: Heap size is 3 = k. Compare new number 5 with root 4. 5 > 4, so we should replace. Pop root 4, push 5. But after popping 4, heap becomes [5,8] temporarily, then push 5 gives [5,5,8] as min-heap? Actually, heapreplace does pop then push in one op. Starting heap [4,5,8] (min-heap order: 4 at root, then 5 and 8). heapreplace with 5: pop 4, push 5, heapify -> new heap [5,5,8] with root 5. So root becomes 5. Wait, but in the problem, we only replace if new number > root. Here 5 > 4, so yes replace. But let's check: original heap had [4,5,8], after adding 5, we have [5,5,8] with root 5. This is correct because the top 3 numbers now are 5,5,8, and 3rd largest is 5.
Heap size = 3 = k. Compare new number 5 with root 10. 5 <= 10, so ignore. Heap remains [10,20,30], root=10.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining k Largest Elements with a Min-Heap
ladders.desc: The central technique of using a min-heap of size k to track the k largest elements.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the algorithmic insight: to find the kth largest element dynamically, maintain a min-heap that stores exactly the k largest elements seen so far. The intuition is that the kth largest is the smallest among the top k elements. By using a min-heap, we can efficiently access this smallest (the root) in O(1) time. When a new element arrives, if it is larger than the current kth largest (the root), it should replace the root because the root is the smallest in the top k and thus the one to be bumped out. If the new element is smaller or equal, it cannot be in the top k, so we ignore it. This ensures the heap always contains the k largest elements. The key is that the heap size never exceeds k, saving memory. Step-by-step: initialize empty min-heap. For each new element x: if heap size < k, add x. Else, if x > heap[0], replace heap[0] with x and heapify. The kth largest is heap[0]. Time per element is O(log k) for heap operations. Space is O(k). This logic is the heart of L1 and L0. Common mistakes include using a max-heap (which would require storing all elements or complex logic) or not limiting heap size to k. Real code pattern: heap = []; for x in stream: if len(heap) < k: heapq.heappush(heap, x); elif x > heap[0]: heapq.heapreplace(heap, x).

examples.input: k=2, elements so far: [5,1,3], heap currently [1,5] (min-heap, root=1), new element=4
examples.trace: Current heap has size 2 = k. Root is 1 (current 2nd largest). New element 4 > root 1, so replace root with 4. After heapreplace, heap becomes [4,5]? Wait, but let's simulate: original elements sorted: [1,3,5], top 2 are [3,5]. After adding 4, top 2 should be [4,5]. Starting heap [1,5] (min-heap order: 1,5). Actually, with elements [5,1,3], after processing 5 and 1, heap would be [1,5]. Then processing 3: since heap size is 2 = k, compare 3 with root 1, 3 > 1, replace -> heap becomes [3,5]. So before new element 4, heap is [3,5] with root 3. Now for new element 4: compare 4 > root 3, replace root 3 with 4 -> heap becomes [4,5], root 4. Yes.

examples.input: k=3, elements so far: [10,20,30], heap [10,20,30], new element=25
examples.trace: Heap size 3 = k. Root is 10. New element 25 > 10, so replace root 10 with 25. After heapreplace, heap becomes [20,25,30] (min-heap order), root 20.

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Heap Operations for Dynamic Data
ladders.desc: Understanding heap push, pop, and replace operations to maintain a min-heap with streaming data.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the heap data structure operations essential for the algorithm. A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is smaller than or equal to its children, allowing O(1) access to the minimum element (root) and O(log n) insertion and deletion. For streaming data, we need to add new elements and possibly remove the smallest to keep heap size bounded. Key operations: push (insert) adds a new element and restores heap property by bubbling up; pop removes the root and restores by bubbling down; heapreplace (or combination of pop then push) efficiently replaces the root with a new element. The intuition is that by always removing the smallest (root) when adding a larger element, we maintain a set of the largest elements. Algorithm: to add a number x to a heap of size at most k: if size < k, heapq.heappush(heap, x). If size == k and x > heap[0], heapq.heapreplace(heap, x) (which pops the root and pushes x in one operation, more efficient than separate pop and push). Time complexity: push and replace are O(log k). Space: O(k). This building block is used in L2 to implement the core logic. Common mistakes include using heap[0] to access root without checking if heap is empty, or misunderstanding heapreplace (it assumes heap is non-empty). Real code pattern: import heapq; heap = []; heapq.heappush(heap, 5); min_val = heap[0]; heapq.heappop(heap); heapq.heapreplace(heap, 10).

examples.input: Heap initially [3,7,10] (min-heap), perform heapreplace with new value 5
examples.trace: Starting heap as min-heap: root is 3, children 7 and 10. heapreplace pops the root 3, then pushes 5. After pop, heap becomes [7,10] temporarily, then push 5: add 5 at end, bubble up: compare 5 with parent 10, swap? Actually, in array representation: initial [3,7,10]. heapreplace: pop 3, last element 10 moves to root -> [10,7], then bubble down: compare 10 with children 7 and (none), swap with 7 -> [7,10]. Then push 5: add to end -> [7,10,5], bubble up: compare 5 with parent 10, swap -> [7,5,10], then compare 5 with parent 7, swap -> [5,7,10]. Final heap [5,7,10].

examples.input: Empty heap, push 4, then push 2, then push 6
examples.trace: Start empty. Push 4: heap [4]. Push 2: add to end [4,2], bubble up: compare 2 with parent 4, swap -> [2,4]. Push 6: add to end [2,4,6], bubble up: compare 6 with parent 4, no swap. Final heap [2,4,6].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Swapping in a Heap
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of comparing elements and swapping to maintain heap property.
ladders.explanation: This level explains the basic mechanics of heap operations: comparing elements and swapping them to preserve the min-heap property (parent <= children). In a min-heap represented as an array, for an element at index i, its parent is at (i-1)//2, left child at 2*i+1, right child at 2*i+2. When inserting a new element, we add it at the end and 'bubble up' by comparing with its parent and swapping if smaller, repeating until heap property is restored. When removing the root, we swap it with the last element, remove it, and 'bubble down' the new root by comparing with its children and swapping with the smaller child if larger, repeating. These compare-and-swap operations are O(log n) in the worst case. The intuition is that by always moving the smallest element upward or the larger downward, we maintain the min-heap structure efficiently. This basic operation is used in L3 for heap push/pop. Common mistakes include incorrect index calculations or not handling edge cases like empty heaps. Real code pattern for bubble up: while i > 0 and heap[i] < heap[parent]: swap(heap[i], heap[parent]); i = parent. For bubble down: while True: smallest = i; if left < n and heap[left] < heap[smallest]: smallest = left; similarly for right; if smallest != i: swap(heap[i], heap[smallest]); i = smallest; else break.

examples.input: Heap array [3,7,10,8], bubble up the last element (index 3, value 8)
examples.trace: Start with heap [3,7,10,8]. Last element index 3, value 8. Parent index = (3-1)//2 = 1, value 7. Compare 8 with 7: 8 >= 7, so no swap. Heap remains [3,7,10,8].

examples.input: Heap array [5,10,15,20,25], bubble down the root (index 0, value 5) after a pop operation simulation
examples.trace: Assume we popped root 5, and last element 25 moved to root: heap [25,10,15,20]. Now bubble down root 25. Index 0, left child index 1 value 10, right child index 2 value 15. Find smallest child: min(10,15)=10. Compare 25 with 10: 25 > 10, so swap with left child: heap becomes [10,25,15,20]. Now at index 1, left child index 3 value 20, right child index 4 (out of bounds). Compare 25 with 20: 25 > 20, swap: heap becomes [10,20,15,25]. Now at index 3, no children. Stop. Final heap [10,20,15,25].

testCases.input: Heap [2,5,9], bubble up new element 1 added at end
Heap [4,6,8], compare root 4 with children 6 and 8
Heap [10,20,30,40], bubble down root 10 after hypothetical replacement
Heap [3,1,2], bubble up element 1 at index 1
Heap [6,2,4,8], bubble up element 2 at index 1
Heap [1,3,2,4], bubble up element 4 at index 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Heap?
ladders.desc: Introduction to heap data structure as a complete binary tree with heap property.
ladders.explanation: A heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property. For a min-heap, each parent node is less than or equal to its child nodes. It is typically implemented as an array representing a complete binary tree, where for any index i, parent is at (i-1)//2, left child at 2*i+1, and right child at 2*i+2. The heap property ensures the minimum element is always at the root (index 0), allowing O(1) access. Heaps are used for efficient priority queue operations. The intuition is that by organizing data in this way, we can quickly add or remove elements while keeping the smallest (or largest for max-heap) readily available. This foundation is prerequisite for L4. Common mistakes include confusing heap with binary search tree or not understanding the array representation. Real code pattern: heap = [] (list in Python, with heapq module providing operations). Example: heap = [2,3,5] represents a min-heap with root 2, left child 3, right child 5.

examples.input: Array [4,10,8,15,20] - check if it satisfies min-heap property
examples.output: No, because 10 > 8 (child), but let's verify: index 0:4, children at 1:10 and 2:8. Min-heap requires parent <= children: 4 <=10 yes, 4<=8 yes. But for index 1:10, children at 3:15 and 4:20, 10<=15 yes, 10<=20 yes. So it actually does satisfy? Wait, 10 and 8 are both children of 4, they don't need to be ordered between themselves. So yes, it is a min-heap. Root 4 is smallest.
examples.trace: Check each parent: index 0 value 4, children indices 1 and 2 values 10 and 8: 4 <=10 and 4<=8, ok. Index 1 value 10, children indices 3 and 4 values 15 and 20: 10<=15 and 10<=20, ok. Index 2 value 8, no children. So it is a valid min-heap.

Start with empty, add 7: [7]. Add 3: [7,3], bubble up: compare 3 with parent 7, swap -> [3,7]. Add 9: [3,7,9], bubble up: compare 9 with parent 3, no swap. Add 1: [3,7,9,1], bubble up: compare 1 with parent 7, swap -> [3,1,9,7], then compare 1 with parent 3, swap -> [1,3,9,7]. Final heap [1,3,9,7].

testCases.expected: No (1 is child of 2 but smaller)

testCases.input: Parent of element at index 3 in a heap
Left child of index 1 in a heap
Right child of index 0 in a heap
Array representation of heap with elements [10]

approaches.name: Max-Heap O(n log n) per query
approaches.summary: Store all stream elements in a max-heap. For each query to find the kth largest, repeatedly pop k-1 elements to remove the largest ones, then peek the root for the kth largest, and push back the popped elements. This is less efficient for queries but uses a different heap structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Kth Largest in Infinite Stream using Max-Heap
ladders.desc: Implement a class that maintains a max-heap of all stream elements and returns kth largest on query by temporarily removing k-1 largest elements.
ladders.explanation: This solution addresses the core challenge of finding the kth largest element in an infinite stream where elements arrive continuously and queries can occur at any time. The intuition is to maintain all elements in a max-heap, which naturally organizes elements with the largest at the root. When a query for the kth largest arrives, we need to temporarily access the element that would be at position k if the stream were sorted in descending order. Since a max-heap only gives direct access to the largest element (root), we pop k-1 elements to remove the largest ones, then peek at the new root which becomes the kth largest. Crucially, we must push back all popped elements to preserve the heap for future queries and stream additions. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for frequent queries because each query requires O(k log n) operations (where n is current heap size). The algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty max-heap. 2. For add(val): push -val (using min-heap as max-heap in Python). 3. For getKth(k): if k > heap size, return error; create temporary list; pop k-1 elements, storing them; peek root for kth largest; push all stored elements back; return kth largest. Time: O(n log n) per query due to k up to n. Space: O(n) for heap storage. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of temporarily removing and restoring elements. Common mistakes: Not pushing back popped elements (corrupts heap), forgetting to handle k > size, using wrong heap type (max vs min). Real code pattern: Use heapq with negative values for max-heap.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty max-heap (using heapq with negatives). Step 2: Add 5: heap = [-5]. Step 3: Add 3: push -3, heap = [-5, -3]. Step 4: Add 8: push -8, heap = [-8, -3, -5]. Step 5: Add 1: push -1, heap = [-8, -3, -5, -1]. Step 6: getKth(2): k=2, size=4 valid. Pop k-1=1 element: pop gives -8 (value 8), store [8]. Heap now [-5, -3, -1]. Peek root: -5 (value 5) is 2nd largest. Push back stored 8: push -8, heap = [-8, -3, -1, -5]. Return 5.

Step 1: Initialize heap. Step 2: Add 10: heap=[-10]. Step 3: Add 20: heap=[-20,-10]. Step 4: Add 30: heap=[-30,-10,-20]. Step 5: Add 40: heap=[-40,-30,-20,-10]. Step 6: getKth(4): k=4, size=4. Pop 3 elements: pop -40 (40), store [40]; pop -30 (30), store [40,30]; pop -20 (20), store [40,30,20]. Heap now [-10]. Peek root: -10 (10) is 4th largest. Push back: push -20, push -30, push -40, heap=[-40,-30,-20,-10]. Return 10. Step 7: Add 5: push -5, heap=[-40,-30,-20,-10,-5]. Step 8: getKth(3): k=3, size=5. Pop 2 elements: pop -40 (40), store [40]; pop -30 (30), store [40,30]. Heap now [-20,-10,-5]. Peek root: -20 (20) is 3rd largest. Push back: push -30, push -40, heap=[-40,-30,-20,-10,-5]. Return 20.

testCases.expected: Error: k larger than stream size

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Temporarily Remove and Restore k-1 Largest Elements
ladders.desc: Implement function that pops k-1 elements from max-heap, peeks root, and pushes elements back.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the query operation in the full problem. The intuition is that to find the kth largest in a max-heap, we need to temporarily eliminate the k-1 larger elements that obscure it. By popping them, we bring the kth largest to the root position where it can be accessed via peek. However, we must restore the heap to its original state (except order may vary) for future operations, so we push back all popped elements. This ensures the heap remains a valid data structure containing all stream elements. The algorithm: 1. Check if k <= heap size, else error. 2. Initialize empty list 'popped'. 3. For i from 1 to k-1: pop from heap, store value in popped. 4. Peek root (without popping) to get kth largest. 5. For each value in popped: push back to heap. 6. Return kth largest. Time: O(k log n) for pops and pushes. Space: O(k) for storing popped elements. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called in getKth() method. Connection to L2: It relies on heap peek/pop/push operations. Common mistakes: Not storing popped values correctly, pushing back in wrong order (order doesn't matter for heap), modifying heap before error check. Real code pattern: Use heapq.heappop() and heapq.heappush().

examples.trace: Step 1: k=3, size=4 valid. Step 2: popped=[]. Step 3: Pop k-1=2 elements: pop gives -8 (8), popped=[8]; heap now [-5,-1,-3]. Pop gives -5 (5), popped=[8,5]; heap now [-3,-1]. Step 4: Peek root: -3 (3) is 3rd largest. Step 5: Push back: push 8 as -8, heap=[-8,-1,-3]; push 5 as -5, heap=[-8,-5,-3,-1]. Return 3.

Step 1: k=2, size=3 valid. Step 2: popped=[]. Step 3: Pop k-1=1 element: pop gives -10 (10), popped=[10]; heap now [-9,-8]. Step 4: Peek root: -9 (9) is 2nd largest. Step 5: Push back: push 10 as -10, heap=[-10,-9,-8]. Return 9.

testCases.expected: Error: k larger than heap size

ladders.title: Core Logic — Accessing kth Largest via Temporary Removal in Max-Heap
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the insight that popping k-1 elements reveals kth largest at root.
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains why temporarily removing elements works to find the kth largest. The intuition stems from the max-heap property: the largest element is always at the root. After removing the largest (via pop), the next largest moves to the root. Therefore, if we repeatedly pop k-1 times, we remove the k-1 largest elements, causing the kth largest to bubble up to the root position. At this point, we can peek at the root without popping to get our answer. This is a direct application of heap ordering. The key is that pops are temporary—we restore them afterward to maintain the dataset. The algorithm concept: 1. Start with max-heap of n elements. 2. For i=1 to k-1: pop heap (removes current largest). 3. After k-1 pops, root is kth largest. 4. Peek root. 5. Restore popped elements. Time: O(k log n) for pops. Space: O(1) if not counting storage for restoration. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the subroutine. Connection to L3: It assumes ability to pop and peek heap. Common mistakes: Thinking kth largest requires sorting, not realizing heap property enables this, confusing with min-heap. Real code pattern: Conceptual without full implementation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap: root 9 (largest). Step 2: Pop k-1=1 element: remove 9, heap reorganizes with new root 7 (now largest). Step 3: After pop, root is 7, which is 2nd largest in original set. Step 4: Peek root: 7. Step 5: Restore 9.

Step 1: Initial heap: root 6. Step 2: Pop 1st element: remove 6, heap becomes [4,0,2] (root 4). Step 3: Pop 2nd element: remove 4, heap becomes [2,0] (root 2). Step 4: After 2 pops, root is 2, which is 3rd largest originally. Step 5: Peek root: 2. Step 6: Restore 4 and 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Max-Heap Peek, Pop, and Push Operations
ladders.desc: Implement and analyze fundamental max-heap operations: access root, remove root, insert element.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers the essential heap operations required for the core logic. A max-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is greater than or equal to its children, ensuring the maximum element is at the root. Peek (or find-max) returns the root value in O(1) time without modifying the heap. Pop (or extract-max) removes the root, which involves: 1. Save root value. 2. Move last element to root. 3. Heapify down (sift-down) to restore heap property by swapping with larger child until heap order is satisfied, taking O(log n) time. Push (or insert) adds a new element: 1. Place element at end. 2. Heapify up (sift-up) by swapping with parent if larger, until heap property holds, taking O(log n) time. The intuition is that heap structure allows efficient maintenance of partial order. These operations are implemented using an array representation where parent index i has children at 2i+1 and 2i+2. Time: Peek O(1), Pop O(log n), Push O(log n). Space: O(1) per operation. Connection to L2: These operations enable temporary removal and restoration. Connection to L4: They rely on array indexing and comparisons. Common mistakes: Not heapifying after pop/push, incorrect array indices, confusing max-heap with min-heap. Real code pattern: Use heapq module with negatives or implement manually.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap array: [9,7,5,3,2]. Step 2: Pop: save root 9. Step 3: Move last element 2 to root: [2,7,5,3]. Step 4: Heapify down: compare root 2 with children 7 and 5, swap with larger child 7: [7,2,5,3]. Compare 2 with children (none), stop. Step 5: Return 9, heap is [7,2,5,3] (visualized as tree: root 7, children 2 and 5, 2's child 3).

Step 1: Initial heap: [8,4,6]. Step 2: Push 10: add at end: [8,4,6,10]. Step 3: Heapify up: index 3 (10) parent index (3-1)//2=1 value 4, 10>4 so swap: [8,10,6,4]. Parent of index 1 is index 0 value 8, 10>8 so swap: [10,8,6,4]. Step 4: Heap is [10,8,6,4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing for Binary Heap Representation
ladders.desc: Implement functions to navigate parent-child relationships in heap array.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is foundational for implementing heap operations. A binary heap is typically stored as an array where the tree structure is implicit. For a 0-indexed array: the parent of element at index i is at floor((i-1)/2); the left child is at 2i+1; the right child is at 2i+2. This representation saves space and allows efficient access. The intuition is that the array order corresponds to a level-order traversal of the complete binary tree. These indexing functions are used in heapify operations to compare and swap elements. For example, when heapifying down after a pop, we start at root index 0, compare with children at indices 1 and 2, and swap if needed. The algorithm for parent(i): return (i-1)//2 (integer division). For left(i): return 2*i+1. For right(i): return 2*i+2. Time: O(1) per index calculation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: These are used in heapify steps. Connection to L5: They rely on array concepts. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors, using 1-indexing, not handling indices out of bounds. Real code pattern: Simple arithmetic functions.

examples.trace: Step 1: Given index i=2. Step 2: parent = (2-1)//2 = 1//2 = 0. Step 3: left = 2*2+1 = 4+1 = 5. Step 4: right = 2*2+2 = 4+2 = 6. So parent index 0, left child index 5, right child index 6.
Step 1: i=0. Step 2: parent = (0-1)//2 = -1//2 = -1 (indicating no parent, as root). Step 3: left = 2*0+1 = 1. Step 4: right = 2*0+2 = 2. So no parent, left child index 1, right child index 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a Max-Heap as a Data Structure
ladders.desc: Define and visualize a max-heap as a complete binary tree with parent >= children.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces the max-heap, a prerequisite for the problem. A max-heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies two properties: 1. It is a complete binary tree (all levels are filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right). 2. The heap property: each node is greater than or equal to its children (for max-heap). This ensures the maximum element is always at the root. The intuition is that it prioritizes quick access to the largest element while maintaining efficient insertions and deletions. Unlike a sorted array, a heap does not keep all elements in full order, but this partial order allows O(log n) updates. It is commonly used in priority queues. Visualize as a tree: e.g., elements [9,5,7,2,3] form a tree with root 9, left child 5, right child 7, 5's children 2 and 3. Array representation stores elements in level-order: [9,5,7,2,3]. Key points: root is largest, operations like insert and remove-max maintain heap property through swaps. Time: Access max O(1), insert O(log n), remove-max O(log n). Space: O(n) to store elements. Connection to L4: This concept uses array for storage. Common mistakes: Confusing with min-heap, not ensuring complete tree, thinking it's fully sorted. Real code pattern: Conceptual definition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Ensure heap property: build tree: root 8, left child 3, right child 6, 3's children 1 and 2. Step 2: Check: 8>=3 and 8>=6, 3>=1 and 3>=2, so valid max-heap. Step 3: Array representation: level-order gives [8,3,6,1,2].

Step 1: Build tree: root 5, left child 10, right child 7. Step 2: Check heap property: root 5 is not >= children 10 and 7 (fails because 5<10). Step 3: This is not a valid max-heap; would need to swap 5 and 10 to fix.

approaches.name: Two Heaps O(log n) per insertion
approaches.summary: Adapt the median-finding two-heap technique: maintain a min-heap for elements larger than the kth largest and a max-heap for smaller ones, balancing to keep the kth largest accessible. This allows dynamic updates and queries but is more complex to implement.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) per insertion, O(1) per query

ladders.title: Full Problem — Kth Largest in Infinite Stream with Two Heaps
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding kth largest element in a continuous data stream using min-heap and max-heap approach.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires maintaining the kth largest element in an infinite stream where elements arrive one by one. The two-heap approach adapts the median-finding technique: we maintain a min-heap for elements larger than the kth largest and a max-heap for elements smaller than or equal to the kth largest. The intuition is that by keeping exactly k elements in the max-heap (or k-1 in max-heap plus 1 in min-heap), the largest element in the max-heap (which is a max-heap) will be the kth largest overall. When a new element arrives, we compare it with the current kth largest. If it's larger, it goes to the min-heap; if smaller or equal, it goes to the max-heap. Then we rebalance to maintain the invariant that the max-heap contains the k largest elements (or k-1 elements with the kth in min-heap). The query for kth largest is O(1) by peeking at the appropriate heap root. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty max-heap and min-heap. 2. For each new element: a. If max-heap size < k, push to max-heap. b. Else compare with max-heap root (current kth largest). If smaller, push to max-heap; else push to min-heap. c. Rebalance: If max-heap size > k, move root to min-heap. If max-heap size < k and min-heap not empty, move min-heap root to max-heap. 3. Query: If max-heap size == k, return max-heap root; else return min-heap root. Time: O(log n) per insertion due to heap operations. Space: O(n) to store all elements. Connection to L1: This uses the rebalancing subroutine from L1. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle initial k elements specially, incorrect comparison logic, or improper rebalancing conditions. Real code pattern: Use heapq for min-heap and negate values for max-heap in Python.

examples.trace: Initialize: max=[], min=[]. Add 5: size<k, add to max → max=[5], kth=5. Add 2: size<k, add to max → max=[5,2], root=5, kth=5. Add 8: size=2<k, add to max → max=[5,2,8], root=8, kth=8. Add 1: size=k, compare 1 with root(8): 1 ≤ 8, ignore → max=[8,5,2], kth=2. Add 9: compare 9 > root(2)? Yes, pop 2, add 9 → max=[9,8,5], min=[2], kth=5. Add 4: compare 4 ≤ root(5)? Yes, ignore → max=[9,8,5], kth=5. Add 7: compare 7 > root(5)? Yes, pop 5, add 7 → max=[9,8,7], min=[2,5], kth=7. Add 3: compare 3 ≤ root(7)? Yes, ignore → kth=7. Add 6: compare 6 ≤ root(7)? Yes, ignore → kth=7. Final: max=[9,8,7], kth=7 ✓

Initialize: max=[], min=[]. Add 10: size<k, add to max → max=[10], kth=10. Add 20: size<k, add to max → max=[20,10], root=20, kth=10. Add 30: size=k, compare 30 > root(10)? Yes, pop 10, add 30 → max=[30,20], min=[10], kth=20. Add 40: compare 40 > root(20)? Yes, pop 20, add 40 → max=[40,30], min=[10,20], kth=30. Add 50: compare 50 > root(30)? Yes, pop 30, add 50 → max=[50,40], min=[10,20,30], kth=40. Final: max=[50,40], kth=40 ✓

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Balancing Two Heaps for Kth Largest
ladders.desc: Implement the rebalancing logic that maintains the invariant for kth largest element between a max-heap and min-heap.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine ensures the invariant after insertion: max-heap size = k (once filled) and max-heap root is the kth largest. The algorithm is simpler than stated: (1) When new_element arrives and max_size < k, add it to max. (2) When max_size = k, compare new_element with max_root. If new_element > root, pop the root (it moves to min-heap implicitly) and push new_element to max. If new_element ≤ root, ignore it. This maintains max_size = k after the initial phase. The key insight: the max-heap (via negation) stores the k largest elements with its root being the smallest of them = kth largest. No additional "rebalancing" between heaps is needed if we enforce the comparison rule. Each heap operation is O(log n). Connection to L0: This logic is applied when add() is called. Common mistakes: Incorrect comparison logic, pushing to the wrong heap, or not maintaining max_size = k. Real code pattern: if max_heap_size < k: heappush(max_heap, -num); elif num > -max_heap[0]: heapreplace(max_heap, -num).

examples.trace: Initial: max size=3, min size=2. Add 4: compare 4>1 (max root), so push to min: min=[4,8,9]. Now max=[5,2,1], min=[4,8,9]. Rebalance: max size=3=k, no move. But wait, we need max to contain k largest: currently max has [5,2,1], min has [4,8,9]. The 3 largest are 9,8,5, but max has 5,2,1. Actually, after adding 4 to min, we should rebalance: max size=3, but 1 is not among top 3. So we move max root 1 to min: pop max gives 1, push to min: min=[1,4,8,9], max=[5,2]. Now max size=2<k=3 and min not empty, so move min root 1 to max: pop min gives 1, push to max: max=[5,2,1], min=[4,8,9]. Final: max=[5,4,2] after heapify, min=[8,9,1].

Initial: max size=1, min size=2. Add 15: compare 15>10, push to min: min=[15,20,30]. Now max=[10], min=[15,20,30]. Rebalance: max size=1<k=2 and min not empty, so move min root 15 to max: pop min gives 15, push to max: max=[15,10], min=[20,30]. Final: max=[15,10], min=[20,30].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining K Largest Elements with Two Heaps
ladders.desc: The central technique of using a max-heap for k largest elements and min-heap for others to enable O(1) kth largest queries.
ladders.explanation: This core logic revolves around the insight that to quickly find the kth largest element dynamically, we can partition the stream into two sets: the k largest elements and all others. By storing the k largest elements in a max-heap (where the root is the smallest among them, i.e., the kth largest), and all smaller elements in a min-heap (where the root is the largest among them), we can answer queries in O(1) by looking at the max-heap root. The intuition is that the kth largest element is the minimum of the k largest elements. When a new element arrives, we compare it with the current kth largest (max-heap root). If it's larger, it belongs to the set of k largest, so we insert it into the max-heap; otherwise, it goes to the min-heap. Then, we adjust the heaps to maintain exactly k elements in the max-heap. This ensures that after each operation, the max-heap root always represents the kth largest element. The time complexity per insertion is O(log k) for heap operations, but since k can be up to n, it's O(log n). Space is O(n) for storing all elements. Connection to L1: This logic defines the invariant that the rebalancing subroutine maintains. Common mistakes: Confusing which heap stores larger elements, or misidentifying the kth largest element. Real code pattern: Use negative values for max-heap with heapq.

examples.input: k=3, current: max=[5,2,1] (kth=1), min=[8,9], new element=4
examples.trace: Start: max has 3 largest: 5,2,1 with kth=1. min has larger: 8,9. New element 4: compare with kth=1. 4>1, so it should be among k largest. Add to max: max=[5,2,1,4]. Now max has 4 elements > k=3. The k largest should be 5,4,2? Actually, after adding, the 3 largest are 9,8,5. But max has 5,4,2,1. We need to keep only k=3 largest in max. So remove smallest from max (1) to min: max=[5,4,2], min=[1,8,9]. Now kth is max root=2. Check: 3 largest are 9,8,5? Wait, 5 is in max, 8,9 in min. The kth largest should be 5? Let's list all: [9,8,5,4,2,1]. 3rd largest is 5. But max root is 2. Error: we need max-heap to store k largest, but we have max with [5,4,2] and min with [1,8,9]. The 3 largest are 9,8,5, but max only has 5,4,2. So we should move 9 from min to max? Actually, the logic: max should contain k largest. Currently, max has 5,4,2 (which are not the 3 largest). So we need to rebalance: move max root 2 to min, then move min root 9 to max. Final: max=[9,5,4], min=[8,2,1]. kth=4.

examples.input: k=2, current: max=[10] (kth=10), min=[20], new element=15
examples.trace: Start: max has 1 largest: 10, need k=2. min has larger: 20. New element 15: compare with kth=10. 15>10, so add to max: max=[15,10]. Now max size=2=k, so kth is max root=10. min remains [20]. Check: elements [20,15,10], 2nd largest is 15? Actually, sorted: [20,15,10], 2nd largest is 15. But max root is 10. Error: max should have k largest: 20 and 15. But max has 15 and 10. So we need to move 10 to min and 20 to max. Final: max=[20,15], min=[10]. kth=15.

ladders.title: Building Block — Heap Data Structure Operations
ladders.desc: Understanding and implementing basic heap operations: insert, extract-min/max, and peek.
ladders.explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property: for a min-heap, each node is smaller than or equal to its children; for a max-heap, each node is larger than or equal to its children. This structure allows efficient O(log n) insertion and O(log n) extraction of the minimum (or maximum) element, and O(1) peek at the root. The intuition is that by maintaining partial order, we can quickly access the extreme element without fully sorting. Insertion: Add element at the end, then 'bubble up' by swapping with parent until heap property is restored. Extraction: Remove root, replace with last element, then 'bubble down' by swapping with smaller (for min-heap) child until restored. In practice, heaps are often implemented using arrays where for index i, left child at 2i+1, right at 2i+2, parent at floor((i-1)/2). Time: O(log n) for insert and extract, O(1) for peek. Space: O(n). Connection to L2: Heaps are used to maintain the k largest and other elements efficiently. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in array indices, not maintaining heap property after operations, or confusing min-heap with max-heap. Real code pattern: Python's heapq module provides min-heap operations; for max-heap, negate values.

examples.trace: Start heap: [3,7,5,9,10] as array representing tree: root 3, left 7, right 5, etc. Insert 4: add to end: [3,7,5,9,10,4]. Bubble up: compare 4 with parent at index 2 (value 5). 4<5, so swap: [3,7,4,9,10,5]. Compare 4 with parent at index 0 (value 3). 4>3, stop. Final: [3,4,5,9,10,7]? Wait, after swap, array is [3,7,4,9,10,5]. Then bubble up: 4's parent is at index 0? Actually, index of 4 is 2, parent is floor((2-1)/2)=0, value 3. 4>3, so stop. So final is [3,7,4,9,10,5]. But this violates heap property? Check: root 3, left child 7, right child 4. 3<7 and 3<4, ok. Left subtree: 7 has children 9 and 10, 7<9 and 7<10, ok. Right subtree: 4 has child 5, 4<5, ok. So it's valid.

Start max-heap as min-heap of negatives: [-9,-7,-5,-3,-1] representing values [9,7,5,3,1]. Extract min (which is max of original): remove root -9 (value 9). Replace with last element -1: [-1,-7,-5,-3]. Bubble down: compare -1 with children -7 and -5. -1 > -7? Actually, for min-heap, we swap with smaller child. Children are -7 and -5, smaller is -7. -1 > -7, so swap: [-7,-1,-5,-3]. Compare -1 with children: left child index? At index 1, children at 3 and 4: values -3 and none. -1 > -3, swap: [-7,-3,-5,-1]. Final: [-7,-3,-5,-1] representing [7,3,5,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Swapping Elements in a Heap
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of comparing heap elements and swapping to maintain heap property.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two elements in a heap and swapping them if necessary to preserve the heap property (min-heap or max-heap). In a min-heap, we compare a node with its parent during insertion: if the node is smaller, we swap it with the parent to move it up. During extraction, we compare the new root with its children: if it's larger than the smaller child, we swap it down. For a max-heap, the comparisons are reversed. The intuition is that by repeatedly comparing and swapping, we can efficiently restore order after changes. Step-by-step for bubble-up in min-heap: 1. Start at the inserted node at index i. 2. While i > 0: a. Find parent index p = (i-1)//2. b. If heap[i] < heap[p], swap heap[i] and heap[p], set i = p. c. Else break. For bubble-down: 1. Start at root i=0. 2. While True: a. Find left child l = 2*i+1, right r = 2*i+2. b. Let smallest = i. c. If l < size and heap[l] < heap[smallest], smallest = l. d. If r < size and heap[r] < heap[smallest], smallest = r. e. If smallest != i, swap heap[i] and heap[smallest], set i = smallest. f. Else break. Time: O(log n) as each swap moves one level in the tree. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is the core of heap insert and extract operations. Common mistakes: Incorrect index calculations, forgetting to update indices after swap, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: Simple while loops with comparisons.

examples.input: Min-heap array: [3,7,5,9,10], bubble up element at index 4 (value 10)
examples.trace: Start: heap=[3,7,5,9,10], index i=4, value=10. Parent index p=(4-1)//2=1, value=7. Compare 10 < 7? No, so stop. No swaps needed.

examples.input: Min-heap array: [6,8,7,10,9], bubble down from root after extraction
examples.trace: Assume root removed and replaced with last element 9: heap=[9,8,7,10]. Bubble down: i=0, value=9. Left child l=1 value=8, right r=2 value=7. Smallest is i=0. Compare: 8<9, so smallest=1. 7<8, so smallest=2. Smallest=2 != i, swap heap[0] and heap[2]: heap=[7,8,9,10]. Now i=2, value=9. Left child l=5 out of bounds, stop. Final: [7,8,9,10].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Heaps as Priority Queues
ladders.desc: Introduction to the heap data structure as a way to efficiently access the smallest or largest element.
ladders.explanation: A heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property, making it useful as a priority queue. In a priority queue, elements have priorities, and we want to quickly access the highest (or lowest) priority element. A min-heap allows O(1) access to the minimum element and O(log n) insertion and removal; a max-heap does the same for the maximum. The intuition is that instead of sorting all elements (which takes O(n log n)), a heap maintains partial order for faster operations. Imagine a tournament: to find the winner, you don't need to compare everyone; a bracket system (like a heap) reduces comparisons. Heaps are typically implemented as complete binary trees stored in arrays, where each node's children are at predictable indices. Basic operations: insert adds an element and restores heap property; remove_min removes the root and restores property; peek returns the root without removal. Time complexity: O(log n) for insert and remove, O(1) for peek. Space: O(n). Connection to L4: This foundation leads to implementing compare-and-swap operations. Common mistakes: Thinking heaps are sorted arrays, or confusing with binary search trees. Real code pattern: In Python, use list and manual index calculations or heapq module.

examples.trace: Start empty. Insert 5: heap=[5]. Insert 3: add to end [5,3], bubble up: compare 3<5, swap: [3,5]. Insert 8: add [3,5,8], bubble up: 8>3, no swap: [3,5,8]. Insert 1: add [3,5,8,1], bubble up: compare 1<3, swap: [1,5,8,3]. Bubble up: 1 at index 0, stop. Insert 2: add [1,5,8,3,2], bubble up: compare 2<5, swap: [1,2,8,3,5]. Compare 2<1? No, stop. Final: [1,2,8,3,5].

Start heap=[1,2,8,3,5]. Remove root 1. Replace with last element 5: [5,2,8,3]. Bubble down: compare 5 with children 2 and 8. Smaller child is 2. 5>2, so swap: [2,5,8,3]. Compare 5 with children: left child index? At index 1, children at 3 and 4: values 3 and none. 5>3, swap: [2,3,8,5]. Final: [2,3,8,5].
